title: Fail-closed — security as posture, not feature
subtitle: Safe-state defaults, controls that actually fire, and the gap between built and armed
aigp_intro: Security is this project's center of gravity, and this act trains its core posture: fail-closed — default to the safe state, refuse to start rather than run weak, and prove every control armed, precise, and recoverable. The recurring failure shape is the gap between built and armed: mechanisms proven but never invoked, key material never issued, defaults that silently downgrade, deny rules that would starve the system they protect. In AIGP terms this is the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety lived end to end; NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function (risk treatment carried to completion) and MEASURE function with TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation); ISO/IEC 42001's discipline of controls that operate, not merely exist; and Body of Knowledge Domains III and IV, with one excursion into Domain I.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance

lessons.title: Provenance is not trust: your own stored data can still carry an attack.
lessons.failure: Building the Substrate — BlarAI's persistent semantic memory, which chunks loaded documents and approved conversation turns into an on-disk vector store and retrieves them by meaning on every new prompt — the easy assumption sat right there: this content is the user's own, so it can be waved through as trusted. The journal's design entry names why that is wrong in a way that matters. A document loaded a week ago may have carried a prompt injection (text crafted so a model reads it as instructions) that nobody noticed at the time; a past turn may quote something hostile the user pasted from a page. Stored-by-us is not authored-by-us, and even authored-by-us is not safe-to-obey. So every retrieved chunk runs through the exact Layer 1+2 defences a freshly loaded document gets — forged-delimiter neutralisation and per-load datamarking — and is grounded as data, never as instruction.
evidence: 2026-06-02 — Phase 5: BlarAI remembers now
lessons.lesson: The provenance of data — where it came from, who stored it — is independent of its trustworthiness — whether it is safe to act on. A system that conflates the two builds a laundering path: yesterday's unnoticed injection becomes today's trusted instruction. Retrieved memory therefore gets the same defences fresh input gets, and is grounded as data, never obeyed as instruction, no matter that it came from the system's own database.
lessons.growth: The mechanism underneath is a trust shortcut: 'inside the boundary' quietly becomes 'safe,' because the check you remember running was the one at the door. But storage launders origin — once content sits in your own database, nothing about its bytes records that it arrived from a hostile page, and a system that trusts its own store re-delivers the attack with the store's authority attached. Watch for any pipeline where data crosses from external to internal and the security treatment drops away after the crossing: caches, message queues, memory systems, data lakes. Ask of every consumer, not just every ingress: what happens if this record is malicious? If the answer is 'it cannot be, it is ours,' you have found the laundering path. Apply the same defences at retrieval that you apply at ingestion, because retrieval is just ingestion from your past.
lessons.growth_question: If a poisoned document was stored a week ago and retrieved today, which of your defences actually run on it today?

lessons.aigp_text: This is data governance in the AIGP Body of Knowledge's sense — Domain III's competency for governing the collection and use of data extends to data the system re-consumes from its own stores. The lived near-miss maps to the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: a system is robust only if content cannot escalate from data to instruction by virtue of where it is stored. It also exemplifies the data-governance obligation concept the EU AI Act attaches to high-risk systems: provenance tracking helps only if trust decisions do not collapse into it.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Provenance and trustworthiness are independent properties; apply ingestion-grade defences at every retrieval, including from your own store.

lessons.title: A security design is only as real as the hardware it assumes — verify the silicon, not the spec sheet or your own inference.
lessons.failure: BlarAI's trust root was specified for months on Intel SGX: the canonical use-case document had the Policy Agent completing 'SGX local attestation' before deriving its certificate-authority key. But Intel removed SGX from its client CPUs in 2021, and the target Lunar Lake laptop has none — the design described hardware that was never there, and no code had ever run it. Querying the actual device replaced the phantom: tpmtool reported a TPM 2.0 (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip), manufacturer STMicroelectronics. Two smaller versions of the same error bracketed the rebuild. The agent declared the chip 'not Pluton' from that one manufacturer string and was corrected by the machine's owner — Pluton is present, just not serving as the active TPM. And the signing primitive's NCryptDeleteKey flag was wrong in a way no documentation reading revealed; only the real chip returning NTE_BAD_FLAGS did.
evidence: 2026-06-03 — A trust root built on hardware nobody had checked
lessons.lesson: Spec sheets, inherited designs, and API documentation describe an intended device; a trust root must be built on the measured one. Query the silicon — tpmtool, Device Manager, a key round-trip on the actual chip — before writing a line of trust code. And when the hardware's owner says your description is wrong, believe the owner over your inference.
lessons.growth: Designs rot silently because the document and the hardware version on different schedules: the spec was written against a product line that later changed, and nothing forces the paper to notice. The failure mechanism is inheritance without re-verification — each reader assumes the writer checked, so a claim that was never true anywhere can stay load-bearing for months. You prevent it by treating every hardware assumption in a security design as a claim with an expiry date: before building on it, run one cheap probe on the real device — list its capabilities, exercise the exact API call, round-trip a key. The probe costs hours; building a trust chain on absent silicon costs the chain. And calibrate your evidence sources: a single tool's output string is weaker than the device owner's direct knowledge plus a second instrument, and a returned error code from the real chip outranks any manual.
lessons.growth_question: What evidence do you actually hold that the security hardware your design names exists on the machine that will run it?

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MAP function asks an organization to establish context — the system, its capabilities, and its deployment environment — before risks can be measured or managed; a trust root specified for hardware the deployment environment does not contain is a MAP failure in miniature. The correction — characterize the real device, then re-derive the design, recorded in ADR-018 — exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: security properties must hold on the system as deployed, not as imagined.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Verify security hardware on the actual device before designing on it; a spec-sheet trust root can be a phantom.

lessons.title: Fail-closed is not fail-shut: a deny-by-default control must still enumerate — and prove — the legitimate-allow set.
lessons.failure: BlarAI's 'no external network' guarantee was never enforced in code; it held only because the machine was air-gapped and the source happened not to call out — both of which evaporate the day the system faces the internet. Turning the habit into an egress kill-switch (a control that blocks outbound network traffic), the handoff brief framed it as 'refuse any outbound socket' — and that version would have bricked the app on launch, because the runtime's own inter-process communication is sockets: the Hyper-V vsock channel to the virtual machine, the dev-mode loopback TCP substitute, the launcher's 127.0.0.1:5001 handshake. The operator caught it before it shipped. The control became an allowlist — permit loopback and Hyper-V, deny everything else — and sixteen tests assert both halves: external connects, binds, DNS lookups, and UDP sends refused; a real loopback round-trip, the socketpair the event loop is built on, and loopback DNS still working.
evidence: 2026-06-03 — Fail-closed is not fail-shut: the egress switch that had to stay open
lessons.lesson: A deny-by-default control is only safe if the legitimate-allow set is enumerated and proven. The test must assert the positive path — the legitimate channels still pass — as rigorously as the negative one, because a fail-closed control whose allow-set nobody verified is one launch away from a self-inflicted outage that no headless test would catch.
lessons.growth: Deny-by-default feels finished the moment the deny works, because denying is the part with the security story attached — and that bias is exactly what produces self-inflicted outages. The mechanism: the author's attention follows the threat model, so the allow-set gets enumerated from memory rather than from the system's real traffic, and the first launch discovers a legitimate channel nobody listed. Prevent it by inventorying actual legitimate flows before writing the rule — read the code that opens connections, and trace where the control sits relative to wrapping layers (this guard had to bite at the raw socket's connect and bind, before the TLS wrap) — then write the positive-path tests first. A useful self-check for any restrictive control you ship: can you name everything it must still allow, and does a test fail if one of those stops working?
lessons.growth_question: Your new deny-by-default control blocks everything it should — what proves it still permits everything the system itself needs to run?

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function is the home of TEVV — test, evaluation, verification, and validation — and this lesson is TEVV applied to a security control itself: verifying the deny fires is half the work; validating that the system still does its real job under the control is the other half. The arc also exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety, where safety includes the system's own availability: a control that bricks the system it protects is not a safe state.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Deny-by-default requires a proven allow-set: test the positive path as rigorously as the deny, or the control becomes the outage.

lessons.title: A capability proven is not a control enforced — a finding closes when production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone.
lessons.failure: The TPM signing mechanism — the Policy Agent minting its JSON Web Tokens with a non-exportable key inside the TPM (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip) — was built and proven on the real silicon a full increment before the audit's single Critical finding actually closed. In between, nothing an auditor would credit had changed: the config still named the cleartext key, the key still sat in git, and a software-key fallback still existed to silently regress to. Closing the finding was the unglamorous half: repoint the production entrypoint from from_key_file to from_tpm, move the three validators to the chip's exported public key, delete certs/pa_private.pem by git rm (history kept at 95c4f19 — the rotation makes the old key worthless, so no destructive rewrite was needed), and remove the fallback so preflight is fail-closed-until-provisioned with nothing weaker left to choose.
evidence: 2026-06-03 — Closing the Critical: the signing key the model trusts is no longer in git
commit 23b2802
lessons.lesson: Building the strong path is the satisfying half; a security finding stays open until production points at the strong path and the weak one is gone — config repointed, the old secret removed, the fallback deleted. Two governance calls ride with it: key provisioning is a deliberate, witnessed human ceremony, and a machine-bound public key is documented by its fingerprint, not committed as one chip's bytes.
lessons.growth: The mechanism behind half-closed findings is that engineering effort and risk reduction peak at different moments: the interesting work is the new mechanism, but the risk only moves when the old path stops being reachable. Between those two moments the system has both paths, and 'both' means an attacker — or a careless config — still gets the weak one. Train yourself to define closure from the auditor's chair before you start: what evidence would convince a skeptic that the weak path is unreachable? Usually three concrete artifacts — production configuration naming only the strong path, the old credential gone or worthless, and a test that fails if a fallback reappears. Then sequence the unglamorous rotation work as part of the same deliverable, not a follow-up ticket, because follow-up tickets are where weak paths retire to live forever.
lessons.growth_question: The strong mechanism works — but if you audited production today, which path would the config actually choose, and what could it still fall back to?

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function covers prioritizing and responding to risks — treatment plans and their implementation — and this lesson is the difference between a treatment designed and a treatment implemented: residual risk is unchanged until the weak path is gone. The witnessed provisioning ceremony and fingerprint-not-key documentation exemplify the accountability the OECD principles ask for: a human can say who created the trust anchor, when, and how to re-verify it.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: A finding closes when production uses the strong path and the weak one is unreachable — mechanism-built is not risk-moved.

lessons.title: A prefix or allow/deny check on a path you did not canonicalize is a bypass waiting to be typed.
lessons.failure: The Policy Agent's deterministic gate denied restricted file paths with startswith on the raw resource string — deny /etc/, another user's /home/, /internal/. The #5 quality benchmark found the hole: a request for /home/user/../../etc/passwd walked straight through, because the string literally starts with the allowed /home/user/ and does not start with /etc/ — yet the .. segments ('go up one directory') resolve it to /etc/passwd, the very file the rule exists to deny. The deterministic gate returned None and handed the decision to the LLM (large language model) classifier — exactly the layer the pre-filter exists not to depend on. The fix normalized with posixpath, not os.path — which on the Windows host would rewrite / to \ and break the very check being hardened — and checks BOTH the raw and normalized forms so the hardening is provably additive. Four traversal tests were proven red against the pre-fix source.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The bypass that started with the allowed prefix
lessons.lesson: The string a caller types and the file the kernel opens are different objects; a security comparison must run on the canonical form, not the typed one. Normalize an input to the grammar it was written in, and prefer an additive fix — check both raw and normalized forms — over a tidier rewrite that might open a new hole while closing this one.
lessons.growth: Path-traversal bypasses keep recurring because string checks feel like file checks: the rule reads as 'deny /etc/' but executes as 'deny strings beginning with those five characters,' and those are different predicates the moment the input language allows aliases — .. segments, doubled slashes, symlinks, percent-encoding, case folding. Any time you compare attacker-influenced input against an allow or deny list, ask what transformations the consumer applies after your check, because every one of them is an alias an attacker can type. Canonicalize first, compare second — and canonicalize in the input's own grammar, since normalizing POSIX paths with Windows rules silently corrupts the comparison. Then prove the fix with adversarial cases run red against the pre-fix code, plus regression cases showing the old catches still catch: a hardening you cannot show is additive may be a hole you just relocated.
lessons.growth_question: Your check denies the string '/etc/' — what does the system that finally opens the file do to that string after your check has passed it?

lessons.aigp_text: The benchmark that surfaced this — an adversarial corpus run against the gate — is what NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function and its TEVV discipline (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) ask for: probing a control with the inputs an attacker would choose, not the inputs the author imagined. The bypass itself is a robustness failure in the OECD sense of robustness, security and safety: a guardrail that holds only for well-formed input is not a guardrail. The additive raw-plus-normalized fix models risk treatment that cannot regress what it hardens.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Canonicalize before you compare: a prefix check on a raw path is theatre, and an adversarial benchmark is what exposes it.

lessons.title: An over-eager control is a regression wearing a security badge — re-enabling a disabled control is not automatically safe.
lessons.failure: The 2026-06-03 security audit said 'wire the inert leakage detector,' and the Tier-1 wave did, with passing tests. Hours later the live screen earned its keep: the operator loaded two documents, asked for a summary, watched an accurate summary stream onto the screen — and then watched it vanish, replaced by a yellow box reading 'Response held by the output validator: LEAKAGE_DETECTED.' Nothing had leaked. The detector measures cosine similarity (a closeness score between texts) between the model's output and the grounded content, flagging matches above 0.85 as verbatim leakage — but a summary of a document is highly similar to that document by construction, so the control fires on exactly the cases the assistant exists to serve. The mocked tests had verified the wiring, not the behaviour; only the real model with real documents caught it. The fix was a revert to inert: off-and-redesign beats on-and-wrong.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The detector that flagged the assistant for doing its job
lessons.lesson: Before enabling a dormant control, verify its precision against the system doing its normal job, not just its presence — 'turn on the thing that ships off' is a claim to be tested, not a safe default. A control that suppresses correct behaviour is a regression wearing a security badge, and off-and-correct beats on-and-wrong.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is a category error: enabling a control feels like restoring intended behaviour, so the only test anyone writes is 'does it now run' — but a detector has two failure modes, and the audit finding only named one. Misses are the security failure; false positives are a product failure that arrives wearing the security team's badge, which makes it politically harder to roll back. Whenever you activate something dormant — a detector, a stricter threshold, an old feature flag — treat it as shipping a brand-new control: measure its false-positive rate on the system's normal, legitimate workload before enforcement, and stage it observe-only first if you can. And notice the deeper design question a high false-positive rate points at: this detector could not distinguish 'answered from the content you gave it' from 'leaked content you did not ask for' — no threshold fixes a control measuring the wrong property.
lessons.growth_question: If this control were perfectly enforced tomorrow, what fraction of the system's normal, correct behaviour would it block?

lessons.aigp_text: This is the verification-versus-validation distinction inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function and its TEVV discipline (test, evaluation, verification, and validation): the wiring was verified — built to spec, the detector runs — but never validated — does the right thing in the real context, where it flags the assistant's core job. The NIST trustworthy-AI characteristic 'valid and reliable' applies to controls as much as models: a control wrong on normal inputs degrades the system it guards, which the OECD robustness, security and safety principle counts as a safety failure.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Validate a control's precision on normal workload before enforcing it; re-enabling a dormant control is shipping a new one.

lessons.title: A safety control that depends on a human remembering to do the right thing is not a control.
lessons.failure: dev-mode — the relaxed posture with ephemeral keys and no hardware trust — shipped as BlarAI's silent default. Every test run and every 'BlarAI works' report therefore validated a configuration that would never ship: green on dev-mode while the production posture failed, which is why agents kept reporting success the operator could not reproduce at his own screen. The Sprint 13 decision walkthrough named the shape: the only thing standing between the project and a false green was someone remembering to test in production posture — and the operator does not run the tests. The first structural fix landed in the same wave (commit ea879ed): the silent default became loud — a boot banner nobody can miss — plus a fail-closed interlock that refuses to run dev-mode while network-facing, built ahead of the risk it guards, with the secure-by-default flip itself staged for the later increment that could land it without breaking the daily launch.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — The anti-pattern the sprint was built to kill, caught inside the sprint
commit ea879ed
lessons.lesson: If the only thing between you and a false green is someone's memory, you have no control. The fix cannot be 'remember to test in production posture'; it must be structural — make the production posture the one the suite verifies by default, and make the wrong posture fail loudly where automation sees it.
lessons.growth: Memory-dependent controls fail by a predictable mechanism: the person who knows the rule is not the person at the keyboard when it matters, and nothing in the system carries the rule across that gap. Defaults are the strongest policy instrument you have, because the default is what every actor who never thought about the question gets — so when a weak posture is the default, every routine action silently votes for it, and the evidence stream (green tests, success reports) describes a system that will never exist in production. Audit your own projects for verbs like 'remember to,' 'make sure you,' 'don't forget' in runbooks and review checklists; each one is a control specification waiting to be made structural. Move the rule into the default, the automated gate, or the refuse-to-start check — places that fire without anyone recalling them.
lessons.growth_question: Walk through your verification pipeline: at which step would anything fail if everyone simply forgot the rule existed?

lessons.aigp_text: AIGP Domain I's competency on establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle is about exactly this: a policy that lives in human memory is not yet a procedure. The structural fix — secure default, loud banner, fail-closed interlock — exemplifies NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function, which seats risk management in organizational structures and processes rather than individual diligence, and ISO/IEC 42001's management-system logic, where controls are operated and checked on the plan-do-check-act cycle instead of recalled.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: A control that depends on someone remembering is no control; encode it as a default, gate, or refuse-to-start check.

lessons.title: A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt that surfaces later as an audit finding.
lessons.failure: The PII (personally identifiable information) credit-card detector's pattern carried the comment '(Luhn-plausible)' — implying the mod-10 checksum that distinguishes plausible card numbers from arbitrary digit runs. The code ran no checksum at all: it matched any 13-to-19-digit run, so order numbers and account IDs would have been redacted as card numbers the moment the redact-at-egress posture switched on. The gap sat between the comment's claim and the code's behaviour — exactly where false confidence lives, because a reader trusts a validation that is not there until an auditor finds it. Sprint 13's first execution agent made the comment true (commit d910739): a real mod-10 gate on both the block and redact paths, a teeth-test reconstructing the exact order-number false positive the old code produced, and the validator registered in a named per-label table so 'what validation actually runs' is visible code, not a parenthetical promise.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — The anti-pattern the sprint was built to kill, caught inside the sprint
commit d910739
lessons.lesson: A comment that claims a property the code does not enforce is governance debt: a reader trusts a validation that is not there, and an auditor eventually finds the gap. Encode the secondary gate a detector needs as something the reader can watch fail — a registered validator, a test — not as a parenthetical promise.
lessons.growth: Comments drift from code because nothing executes them: the interpreter checks the pattern, the suite checks the behaviour, and the claim in the parenthesis is checked by nobody until an audit reads it against the implementation. The deeper mechanism is that documentation often records intent at design time — 'this should be Luhn-gated' — and intent quietly hardens into assertion as readers inherit it. When you review code, treat every property claim in a comment, docstring, or design document as a test that has not been written yet: either the property is enforced somewhere you can point to, or the claim should be deleted or downgraded to a TODO. The same discipline pays at system scale — security postures described in architecture documents deserve a verification trail, because the documents are what assessors, auditors, and your future self will read first and believe.
lessons.growth_question: Pick any property your code's comments claim — what would actually fail today if that property quietly stopped being true?

lessons.aigp_text: The EU AI Act's high-risk obligations include technical documentation that accurately describes the system, and the NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'accountable and transparent' depends on artifacts that say what the system does, not what its authors intended. A comment claiming an unenforced validation is the small-scale failure of both: documentation an assessor would rely on and be misled by. Making the claim executable — a real checksum, a registered validator, a test — turns the documentation back into evidence.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Documentation that claims an unenforced property is misleading evidence; make the claim executable or remove it.

lessons.title: Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text — a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of its source.
lessons.failure: Wiring the AES-256-GCM field cipher (authenticated encryption) into the substrate store, the first instinct was to treat embeddings — the numeric vectors that represent text by meaning — as non-sensitive metadata; they are not human-readable, after all. The operator's kickoff decision was sharper: embeddings are invertible. The vec2text literature shows a 384-dimensional semantic embedding can reconstruct enough of its source text to be a real privacy leak from a stolen disk — encrypting the house while leaving the blueprint in the mailbox. The cost objection dissolved on measurement: decrypt all embeddings once at unlock into an in-RAM matrix, run vector search over plaintext in memory, decrypt only the top-k matched text per query — measured at +0.124 ms per query (0.397 to 0.521 ms median) and 1.45 ms one-time at boot for 107 embeddings, below measurement noise against time-to-first-token on this hardware.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — Encrypt the derived representations, not just the raw text
commit de78cb2
lessons.lesson: A derived representation can carry the sensitivity of its source: a stored embedding is an invertible shadow of the text it encodes, so encrypting the text while leaving the vectors plaintext is an internally inconsistent posture. Price the protection before rejecting it — here a boot-time cache made the overhead immaterial at single-user scale, paid in proportion to corpus size, not per query.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is an intuition mismatch: 'sensitive' gets read as 'human-readable,' so anything that looks like opaque numbers feels safe. But sensitivity follows information content, not legibility — embeddings, search indexes, caches, logs of prompts, even similarity scores can each reconstruct or reveal slices of the data they were derived from. When you classify what needs protection, trace the data-flow graph downstream from every sensitive source and ask of each derived artifact: with this artifact alone plus public tools, what could a motivated attacker recover? If the answer is 'some of the source,' it inherits the source's protection class. Then do the engineering honestly: measure the cost of protecting it rather than assuming it is prohibitive, because the assumed-expensive control is often cheap once you choose where to pay — at boot rather than per query, here.
lessons.growth_question: List everything your system derives from its most sensitive data — which of those artifacts could reconstruct the source, and are they protected like it?

lessons.aigp_text: This is the NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'privacy-enhanced' applied with technical depth: privacy protection that covers raw text but not its derived representations is incomplete by the data's own information content. It also exemplifies AIGP Domain III's data-governance competency — governing the use of data includes the artifacts the system manufactures from it — and the accepted, documented residual (identical filenames share a keyed hash, the price of deduplication) models the honest residual-risk naming NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function expects.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Derived representations inherit their source's sensitivity: an embedding can invert to its text, so encrypt the shadow with the substance.

lessons.title: Tamper-evident is not non-forgeable, and the difference belongs in a test, not a comment.
lessons.failure: Sprint 13 shipped a hash-chained audit stream for Policy-Agent decisions — tamper-evident by construction — with the signer deliberately left a software HMAC stub whose key was recomputable from filesystem state. The hash chain proves records were not altered after the fact; only a signature proves who wrote them — and the system had only the first property while a comment implied the second. The gap closed with a TPM-sealed (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip) ECDSA signer on a dedicated key, BlarAI-Audit-Signing-Key-v1, deliberately separated from the Policy Agent's JWT (JSON Web Token) key so an audit compromise does not also compromise token issuance. The portfolio-valuable part is the contrast test: one test actually forges a record and asserts the weak stub accepts it; its mirror asserts an adversary holding only the key name cannot produce a signature the TPM signer accepts. The follow-up hardened the failure branches: an unprovisioned key now refuses to start rather than authorizing with no audit trail.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — Closing the door between tamper-evident and non-forgeable — then refusing to run blind
commit 871ced7
commit 4c71aad
lessons.lesson: Tamper-evident and non-forgeable are different properties — a hash chain proves records were not altered afterward; only a signature proves who wrote them — and a system can hold one while a comment implies both. Make the security property executable: write the test that forges against the weak path and the mirror that fails to forge against the strong one.
lessons.growth: Security properties blur together because their everyday descriptions overlap — 'the log is protected' covers integrity, authenticity, and availability, three different guarantees with different mechanisms and different attackers. The failure mechanism here was a deliberate stub plus an ambiguous description: the staged plan was sound, but the words around it claimed the destination while the code stood at the waypoint. When you build security in stages, name precisely which property each stage delivers, and prove the boundary in both directions: a test demonstrating what the current stage still permits (the stub accepts a forged record) is as valuable as the test of what it prevents, because it stops anyone — including you in six months — from believing the stronger claim early. A second habit worth stealing: separate keys by duty, so compromising one control never silently compromises its neighbour.
lessons.growth_question: Your audit log is 'protected' — protected against alteration, against forgery, or both, and which test would tell you?

lessons.aigp_text: Record-keeping and logging are among the EU AI Act's named high-risk obligation concepts precisely because the audit trail is what accountability rests on — and an audit trail that is tamper-evident but forgeable gives accountability with an asterisk an adversary can exploit. The contrast tests are NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) applied to the control itself: the claimed security property is demonstrated in code, in both its weak and strong states, rather than asserted in prose.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Integrity and authenticity are separate guarantees: hash chains prove no alteration; signatures prove authorship. Test the difference, both directions.

lessons.title: A break-glass recovery path must be correct by design and proven against a dead chip, never correct by accident.
lessons.failure: The DEK (data encryption key) recovery flow — unseal the key with the offline recovery key when the TPM (Trusted Platform Module) security chip is gone, then re-seal to a new machine — passed its first review green. But it passed by accident: the dev sealer stub happened to raise an exception the code happened to catch, and the real recovery logic leaned on a private helper, _recovery_wrap, that no public, supported entry point exercised. The merge gate sent it back. Round two made the path correct by construction: public unseal_via_recovery and reseal_dek entry points, and a DeadSealer test fixture whose unseal() always raises — driving the recovery code exactly as a genuinely destroyed TPM would, not as a stub that incidentally errors. The tests now assert both ends of the contract: the wrong-recovery-key case fails closed, and the dead-chip-with-correct-key case succeeds end-to-end into a re-sealed keystore.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — One command for the whole encryption spine — and a recovery path that was right only by accident
commit 6017e5c
lessons.lesson: A break-glass path is the one path you cannot rehearse in production and the one you most need to work the first time, so 'passes today' is not the bar — correct by construction, proven against a simulated dead chip, is. Assert both ends: the wrong key fails closed, and the genuine catastrophe with the right key recovers real data.
lessons.growth: Recovery paths rot because nothing exercises them: the happy path runs daily and self-verifies; the catastrophe path runs only on the day everything else has already failed, in front of the person least equipped to debug it. 'Correct by accident' is the signature failure — a stub's incidental exception happens to route execution the right way, so the green test certifies a coincidence. When you build any disaster control — key recovery, backup restore, failover — construct a fixture that IS the disaster (a sealer that always raises, a region that is simply gone) rather than a mock that imitates a symptom, and drive the path through its public, supported entry points, because the private helper your test reaches into is not the code a panicked operator will run. Then keep the rehearsal alive: a recovery proven once and never re-run decays with every refactor.
lessons.growth_question: If your hardware key store died tonight, has the exact sequence the recovery runbook prescribes ever been executed end-to-end — against a chip that is actually dead?

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function includes planning and implementing responses to risks — and a recovery plan whose enabling path has never been driven as the catastrophe would drive it is a treatment that exists on paper. The trustworthy-AI characteristic 'secure and resilient' makes resilience a tested property: the system's ability to recover from a dead trust anchor is demonstrated — fail-closed on the wrong key, successful on the right one — not presumed from a green suite.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Prove break-glass recovery against a simulated dead chip through public entry points; a green test can certify a coincidence.

lessons.title: A factory that reads missing configuration as "dev mode" turns a misconfiguration into a silent downgrade to the weakest posture.
lessons.failure: The independent sprint audit's MINOR-3 finding named a latent failure in the at-rest encryption: the store factories' dev path was not an explicit opt-in but the implicit default for any misconfiguration. With the BLARAI_DEK_KEYSTORE environment variable absent — the pointer to the DEK (data encryption key) keystore — both the session and substrate builders would quietly construct a SoftwareSealer store encrypting under the hard-coded key b"SOFTWARE-SEALER-NOT-A-SECRET-KEY": effectively-plaintext data that looks encrypted. No data was exposed on the live system, because the production boot had set the variable — but the posture was 'missing config reads as dev mode' rather than 'missing config is a misconfiguration,' one accidental env-var drop from a production boot that writes pseudo-plaintext while appearing to encrypt. The fix: the load-bearing session store now refuses the whole boot with StoreProvisioningError; the non-load-bearing substrate degrades loudly with memory disabled; the dev path requires an explicit dev_mode=True from the caller.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — Closing the silent key-substitution door
commit e16450e
lessons.lesson: Make the safe posture the enforced default and require an explicit caller signal to unlock the weak one. The weak posture must be the thing you opt into, never the thing you fall back to — and the right refusal is sized to accountability: a load-bearing store refuses the whole boot; a non-load-bearing one degrades loudly.
lessons.growth: This failure shape is born of developer convenience compounding silently: the dev path exists so work can proceed without provisioning, and the cheapest way to wire it is as the else-branch — which makes every possible misconfiguration route to it. The danger is camouflage: a system that falls back to weak crypto still runs, still 'encrypts,' still passes a glance, so the downgrade produces no signal anywhere. When you design any control with a strong and a weak mode, draw the decision table for every input state — set, unset, malformed, empty — and check which mode each row lands in; any row that lands weak without an explicit, logged opt-in is a latent downgrade. The general principle: absence of configuration is information about a mistake, not a preference — treat it as one, and fail or degrade loudly enough that the mistake gets fixed.
lessons.growth_question: If the one environment variable your encryption depends on were silently dropped, would the next boot refuse — or run, looking encrypted?

lessons.aigp_text: Secure-by-default is the operational face of the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: the system's weakest posture must be deliberately chosen, never accidentally inherited. The fix also exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function in how it treats degraded states — the response to a missing prerequisite is sized to accountability, refusing the load-bearing path and loudly degrading the optional one — and the finding-to-fix arc itself, an independent review surfacing a latent fail-open the builder missed, is the review loop the framework's risk cycle expects.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Missing configuration is a misconfiguration, not a dev-mode vote: the weak posture must be explicit opt-in, never fallback.

lessons.title: A control built but never issued its key material is half-armed, which is to say off.
lessons.failure: The mTLS path (mutual TLS — both ends of a connection prove identity with certificates) in shared/ipc/vsock.py had been production-ready for many sprints: the server SSL context, CERT_REQUIRED, the handshake, all built. What broke the chain was that the certificate files it needed did not exist — certs/pa_server.pem, certs/pa_server_key.pem, and certs/ca.pem were unprovisioned placeholders — and the gateway client at transport.py:416-436 passed no cert paths at all, so even a valid server would have rejected it. A door with all the hardware installed and no key ever cut. The fix (commit bcda777) co-issues the control and its material at one point in startup: per-boot certificates minted fail-closed before any service constructs, an in-memory certificate authority whose private key dies with the process, a 24-hour lifetime, and a production-wiring regression lock that fails if the certs are minted but never handed to the gateway.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — The door that was built but never had a lock
commit bcda777
lessons.lesson: When a security control depends on a provisioning step, the provisioning must fire automatically as part of the same startup path, never sit as a manual prerequisite no code enforces. Co-issue the control and its key material at one point, so the chain can never be half-armed: either both are present, or the boot refuses.
lessons.growth: Half-armed controls accumulate because building and provisioning live in different workstreams: code review sees the mechanism, tests exercise it with fixture material, and the real key, certificate, or token is 'an ops step' that no automated path owns. The system then carries a fully-specified control that has never once operated — and everything still works, because the unprovisioned branch quietly skips it. Hunt for these by asking, for every credentialed control: where does the material come from on a fresh machine, which code path creates it, and what happens on the boot where it is missing? If the answers are 'a runbook,' 'nothing,' and 'it proceeds,' the control is off. The strong pattern is co-issuance — the same startup sequence that constructs the control provisions its material or refuses — plus a wiring test asserting the consumer actually received it.
lessons.growth_question: For each certificate or key your security design names: which code path creates it on a fresh machine, and what happens on the boot where it does not exist?

lessons.aigp_text: ISO/IEC 42001's management-system framing distinguishes documenting a control from operating it: an AIMS (AI management system) expects controls to be implemented and checked in operation, and mTLS code with no issued certificates is a control documented but inoperative. The co-issuance fix exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function — risk treatment implemented all the way into the running system — and the OECD robustness, security and safety principle's demand that protective mechanisms actually function in the deployed configuration.

aigp_frameworks: ISO/IEC 42001 — AIMS (controls operated, not merely documented)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: A credentialed control with no issued material is off: co-issue keys with the control in one fail-closed startup path.

lessons.title: Build the mechanism and pin the sentinel before you throw the switch — an unactivated mechanism is a deferred bug, and the test suite is what defines "safe" before you flip the bit.
lessons.failure: Flipping BlarAI's default from dev-mode to production posture was a breaking change too risky to fire blind: landing it before the signed manifest and the TPM ceremony keys existed would have bricked the daily boot. The staging pattern carried it. One increment (commit cbe5bb7) built the capability and laid four regression locks asserting the current pre-flip behaviour — lock (a) explicitly asserted resolve_dev_mode(HOST) still returns True — so a premature activation would fail the automated suite loudly. The activation increment (commit 94bc828) then threw the switch: the HOST default flipped from True to False in three lines of runtime code, and the same sentinel lock was updated to assert the new behaviour, with new locks covering the explicit BLARAI_DEV_MODE opt-in and its loud INSECURE banner. The activation was safe precisely because the suite had said what safe meant before the bit moved.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — Building the door without opening it
2026-06-06 — Throwing the switch: production becomes the HOST default
commit cbe5bb7
commit 94bc828
lessons.lesson: A breaking change too risky to attempt cleanly tempts you to ship it unguarded or never ship it; both fail. The third path is staging: one increment builds the capability and pins a sentinel asserting the current behaviour, so premature activation fails loudly; a later increment throws the switch and updates the same sentinel. A mechanism built but never made safe to activate is a bug deferred and dressed as caution.
lessons.growth: Risky flips go wrong in two mirrored ways: shipped eagerly, they fire before their prerequisites exist; deferred indefinitely, the unactivated mechanism rots while everyone believes the capability is done. The mechanism underneath is that 'safe to activate' is usually nobody's explicit definition — it lives as a feeling, so neither the eager nor the cautious party can prove their position. The staging pattern converts the feeling into executable state: the sentinel test asserts which side of the switch the system is on, the prerequisite list becomes the activation increment's checklist, and the flip itself shrinks to a small, reviewable diff that updates the sentinel in the same commit. Use it for any default change, posture change, or migration: build, pin the current state, prepare the prerequisites, then flip with the sentinel — and treat any mechanism that has waited unactivated for months as a finding, not a comfort.
lessons.growth_question: For the switch you are afraid to flip: what test today would fail if someone flipped it prematurely — and what list defines when flipping becomes safe?

lessons.aigp_text: Staged activation with a pinned sentinel is release governance in AIGP Domain III's sense — governing the release of the model and system as a controlled, evidenced act rather than an event. The sentinel is NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) used as a change-control instrument: the suite defines and enforces the system's intended state on each side of the change, and ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act discipline shows in the shape — plan the flip, pin the check, act, re-check.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Stage risky flips: build the mechanism, pin a sentinel asserting current state, then activate by updating the sentinel deliberately.

lessons.title: Off-chip structural proofs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value — stub data does not cheapen the structural proof.
lessons.failure: Before the production-posture activation, the Policy Agent would refuse to start at dev_mode=false for three distinct reasons: no Known-Good Manifest path, no TPM (Trusted Platform Module) key name, no JWT (JSON Web Token) certificate-authority path — a cascade wired in config but never verified end-to-end. There were no real Qwen3-14B model weights at build time, and the temptation was to treat that as a deficiency blocking the harness. The staging increment (commit e1e0c1c) rejected the framing: the cascade to de-risk was structural — the manifest exists, the digests dict parses, the value is 64 hex characters, the validation gate accepts the format — provable with a stub manifest and a harness dummy binary whose digest is genuinely computable. Seven tests went green in 0.57 seconds, plus four fail-closed regression locks. What remained for the operator's on-chip ceremony was exactly what cannot be faked: the chip-bound key provisioning and the real-model digest match.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — Testing the door before handing over the key
commit e1e0c1c
lessons.lesson: Off-chip structural proofs with stubs and on-chip ceremony verification are distinct work with distinct value. The instinct to dismiss stubbed proof as fake is wrong: it de-risks every structural surprise before the operator spends scarce ceremony time, so the human-on-hardware session narrows to exactly the part only the silicon can prove.
lessons.growth: The underlying confusion is between fidelity and coverage: a stubbed environment has low fidelity to production, so it feels like it proves nothing — but most of what breaks in a security cascade is structure (paths, formats, parsing, ordering, fail-closed branches), and structure is exactly what stubs prove completely. Meanwhile the scarce resource — a human at the real hardware, a production key ceremony, a maintenance window — should be spent only on what is irreducibly bound to it. When you plan verification for anything that ends in a privileged or physical step, partition the claims first: which would a stub prove identically, and which can only the real environment answer? Push everything in the first set into cheap automated proof, and write down the second set as the ceremony's entire agenda. The day the human sits down, every failure they hit should be one only that day could have found.
lessons.growth_question: Of everything the expensive on-hardware session is scheduled to verify, how much could a stub have proven this afternoon?

lessons.aigp_text: This is TEVV planning under NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function: test, evaluation, verification, and validation work is partitioned by what each environment can actually evidence, so assessment effort lands where it discriminates. It maps to AIGP Domain IV's competency of performing key activities to assess the AI model and system before deployment — and the deliberate division of machine-provable structure from human-witnessed ceremony reflects the accountability emphasis in the OECD principles: the human act is reserved for what genuinely requires a witness.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Partition verification: stubs prove structure cheaply; spend the scarce on-hardware ceremony only on what silicon alone can prove.

lessons.title: Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal decisions — conflating them leaves the real production path unbuilt behind a fully-specified one.
lessons.failure: The first real production boot (dev_mode=False, HOST mode) failed at the transport layer: the vsock channel tried to create an AF_HYPERV socket — the virtual-machine-boundary transport — with a Linux-style (cid, port) address Windows rejects with bind(): bad family. The deeper problem was topological: in HOST mode there is no VM boundary; every service runs on the same Windows machine, and the correct transport is what a signed sprint acceptance criterion had described all along — loopback (127.0.0.1) plus mTLS (mutual TLS, certificate-proven identity on both ends), a path already verified in isolation earlier in the sprint. It was simply never wired into the boot, because 'production' had been conflated with 'AF_HYPERV': the boot's own step message had said 'real AF_HYPERV path,' which had been wrong for months. The fix (commit c92d06d) threaded an explicit host_mode parameter through the stack, with nine new tests locking family, address, and the end-to-end loopback mTLS handshake.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — The transport path that was designed but never wired
commit c92d06d
lessons.lesson: Transport topology and security mechanism are orthogonal: AF_HYPERV belongs to the VM-boundary topology; mTLS is a security property every production channel needs regardless of topology. The right diagnostic for a well-specified, isolation-verified path the boot never invokes is 'built into nothing,' not 'needs redesign' — wire the proven path in rather than redesigning around the wrong default.
lessons.growth: Conflation bugs grow from shorthand: 'production' starts as a bundle of properties — real model, real certificates, real transport, hardened posture — and the bundle hardens into a single mental switch, so choosing one property silently chooses all the others. Here 'production means the VM transport' survived even though the deployed topology had no VM, leaving the actual production path fully specified, separately verified, and connected to nothing. The protection is to factor your configuration space explicitly: list the independent axes (where services run; how they talk; how the channel is secured; which posture is enforced) and check which combinations the system can really express — every axis collapsed into a mode flag is a place where the real deployment can differ from the imagined one. And when you find a verified-but-uninvoked path, resist the redesign reflex: the cheapest correct fix is usually wiring in what was already proven.
lessons.growth_question: The boot log says 'production path' — which independent decisions (topology, transport, security, posture) did that one word silently make for you?

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MAP function is about establishing context — what the system is, where it actually runs, which deployment environment its risks live in — and this failure was a context error carried in a word: the security architecture was specified for a topology the deployment did not have. Untangling the axes so the real production path got built exemplifies MAP feeding MANAGE, and keeping mTLS on either topology reflects the OECD robustness, security and safety expectation that protection follows the system, not the diagram.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Topology and security are separate axes: a security mechanism must ride whatever transport the real deployment uses.

lessons.title: Config-to-provisioner correspondence must be tested as a unit, or the gap surfaces only at production boot.
lessons.failure: The per-boot certificate provisioner had been written with two services in mind — the Policy Agent server and the gateway client. But the Assistant Orchestrator's config named a third cert pair, certs/orch_client.pem and its key, that the mint had never been taught to produce — and the gap surfaced exactly where such gaps do: the Orchestrator's listener failed at live production boot with 'Failed to create server SSL context: [Errno 2] No such file or directory.' The systematic audit that followed — reading every [ipc] section across all three services' default.toml files — found a fourth unminted pair, certs/router_client.pem, waiting to produce the identical failure the moment the Semantic Router connected. The fix (commit 681b5b6) taught the mint all nine per-boot files and added seven tests, including a direct regression lock on the exact load_cert_chain call that failed.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — The config and the mint were speaking different languages
commit 681b5b6
lessons.lesson: Any config that names a file another component must produce needs a test that walks from the config key to that component's output. The durable safety net is not a per-service regression test added each time a gap bites, but a single test traversing every config section, extracting each named path, and asserting the provisioner covers it.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is parallel evolution without a reconciliation step: configs and provisioners are written by different hands at different times, each correct against its own author's mental inventory, and nothing in the build ever holds the two lists side by side. Such correspondence gaps are invisible to component tests — the provisioner provably writes what it writes, the config provably parses — because the defect lives in the relationship, not in either artifact. Hunt for the pattern wherever one part of a system names resources another part creates: certificate paths, queue names, database migrations versus schema expectations, infrastructure outputs versus application settings. For each, write the closure test: enumerate every consumer-side reference mechanically — do not hand-maintain the list, which recreates the same divergence one level up — and assert the producer covers the set. One enumerating test outlives any number of per-incident patches.
lessons.growth_question: Name a file, queue, or key your config references — what test would fail if the component responsible for creating it never did?

The class-level fix, auditing every config against the provisioner and locking the seam with a test, also models the ISO/IEC 42001 management-system habit of correcting the process that produced a nonconformity, not just the instance that surfaced.

lessons.title: Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability, not a uniform property of a component.
lessons.failure: After the dev-to-production key transition, practice-era rows in sessions.db sat encrypted under the old dev key, and the healthy production TPM-sealed key refused them with AES-GCM InvalidTag — the cipher's tamper-and-wrong-key rejection doing its job. But all three bulk readers — list_sessions, get_session_turns, _backfill_empty_titles — called leaf decrypt helpers that raise on failure inside their loops, so the first legacy row aborted the entire sweep and the whole app bricked: every session denied, including the perfectly healthy ones written under the production key. Two Sprint-14 regression tests had locked exactly this behaviour as 'fail-closed.' The fix (commit 145f2b1): bulk loops quarantine the bad row — omit it, log the stable greppable event SESSION_ROW_DECRYPT_QUARANTINE, continue — while single-record paths keep hard-failing; no plaintext returned, no tampered data trusted, only the blast radius changed. The governing decision record, ADR-025 §2.7, was amended to say what the code now does.
evidence: 2026-06-06 — When "fail closed" becomes a door that locks you out of your own house
commit 145f2b1
lessons.lesson: Fail-closed is a property of a call site relative to its unit of accountability. A leaf decryptor is accountable for one row and must raise when that row will not decrypt; a bulk reader is accountable for the health of the whole store and must not let one sick row deny every healthy one. Applying the leaf posture uniformly converts a confidentiality control into an availability attack surface.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is that 'fail-closed' reads like a single virtue, so it gets applied as a blanket — but the CIA triad (confidentiality, integrity, availability) names three properties, and a control tuned only for the first two can become the attack on the third: here a self-inflicted denial of service, the app refusing its owner's own healthy data. When you set a failure posture, first name the unit of accountability at each call site: what did the caller actually ask for, and what does the caller need to stay healthy? A caller that named one record should hear the failure loudly; a caller sweeping everything on behalf of startup should isolate the casualty, log it observably, and serve the rest. And when a passing test locks the bricking behaviour, recognize the test as part of the defect — rewrite it to assert the synthesis, both the quarantine and the still-hard leaf.
lessons.growth_question: When one record in ten thousand fails its integrity check, what should the user lose — that record, or the application?

lessons.aigp_text: The availability leg of the CIA triad names this failure as the security failure it is, and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety carries the same expectation: a safe system keeps serving its legitimate user under partial failure. The NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'secure and resilient' is the synthesis the fix lands on — confidentiality and integrity untouched, no plaintext and no trusted tampered data, while resilience bounds the blast radius of one bad row to that row.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Size failure posture to accountability: leaf calls fail hard; bulk sweeps quarantine — or confidentiality controls become availability attacks.

lessons.title: An integrity check on a multi-file artifact must cover the whole manifest — and reject whatever the manifest omits.
lessons.failure: BlarAI's weight-integrity gate had guarded the Policy Agent and the Assistant Orchestrator since Sprint 12 — and had verified only the primary model binary, openvino_model.bin, against the manifest the whole time. Every other .bin in the model directory — the draft-model weights, tokenizer embeddings, any future shard — loaded unverified, and nothing at all checked for files present on disk but absent from the manifest. The attack path was clean: swap the primary binary to a new filename and drop a payload under the original name, and the boot gate passes without complaint. The fix (commit 59fa496) added verify_all_manifest_entries: hash and compare every entry in the manifest's digests dict, then a second pass rejecting any .bin on disk that the manifest does not name. Eight new tests cover the four paths — all-pass, swapped digest, extra .bin on disk, missing manifest entry.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — Closing the single-file blind spot in weight integrity
commit 59fa496
lessons.lesson: A manifest defines a set: the integrity check must verify every member of that set and assert no extra members exist. Checking less than the manifest claims is a false assurance; checking less than the directory actually contains is a silent injection window.
lessons.growth: Partial integrity checks survive because the first file checked is the one everybody thinks of as 'the model,' and the green check radiates assurance over the whole artifact — the classic gap between what a control covers and what its consumers believe it covers. The logic to internalize is set equality: an integrity claim over a multi-part artifact must hold in both directions — everything claimed must be present and correct (no member unverified), and everything present must be claimed (no stowaway an attacker added). Audit your own gates with two questions: enumerate what the artifact actually contains versus what the check actually reads — any difference is unverified surface; then ask what happens to a file the inventory does not mention — if the answer is 'nothing notices,' you have the injection window. The same discipline applies beyond model weights: container images, dependency trees, plugin directories, backup sets.
lessons.growth_question: Your integrity gate is green — which files in the artifact did it actually hash, and what happens to a file it has never heard of?

lessons.aigp_text: Model weights are the deployed system's most direct integrity surface, and the EU AI Act's high-risk obligations bundle accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity for good reason: an attacker who can swap a weight shard owns the model's behaviour. The whole-manifest sweep exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety applied at model-load time, and the four fail-closed test paths are NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function TEVV evidence (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) that the gate enforces the set in both directions.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Integrity over a multi-file artifact is set equality: verify every manifest member and reject every file the manifest omits.

lessons.title: Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity.
lessons.failure: Sprint 16's dependency stream found the security-critical dependencies wide open: cryptography>=41.0 allowed a five-major-version window, PyJWT>=2.8 permitted a future 3.x with unknown signing semantics, and openvino>=2024.0 allowed a three-year span of the model-loading runtime to change silently underneath the system. The root pyproject.toml had made the call correctly for cryptography back in Sprint 14, but the five service files had never caught up; commit 639c9b0 closed the gap uniformly, tiered by how directly each dependency sits on the trust boundary. The honest part came at the boundary of the fix itself: PEP 508 — the dependency-specification format pyproject.toml uses — simply does not support hash syntax; the hash=sha256 annotations live in pip-tools and uv lock files. So the pins contain version jumps; they cannot stop a malicious wheel served at the same version number. The rationale doc names that gap instead of implying coverage.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — Closing the unbounded upper-bounds on the security surface
commit 639c9b0
lessons.lesson: Version pins are containment, not supply-chain integrity: bounding a dependency stops a silent major-version jump, but a version specifier cannot stop a malicious package served at the same version number — only a hashed lock file closes that gap. The honest posture names the boundary the control actually buys instead of letting a pin masquerade as a guarantee.
lessons.growth: Controls overclaim through vocabulary drift: 'we pinned our dependencies' sounds like supply-chain security, and unless someone prices exactly which attack each measure stops, the team's belief expands to fill the reassuring phrase. The practice to build is threat-by-threat accounting: for each control, write down the attacks it defeats (an upstream publishing a breaking new major version), the attacks it does not (a compromised registry serving altered bytes at the pinned version), and the named next step that would close the gap (a lock file generated with hashes, verified at install). That residual list is not an admission of weakness — it is the most valuable artifact the exercise produces, because the unnamed residual is the one nobody is watching. Apply the same accounting to backups, encryption, and scanning: every control has a boundary, and the danger concentrates wherever the boundary is assumed rather than written.
lessons.growth_question: Your dependencies are pinned — what, precisely, stops a tampered package arriving under the exact version number you pinned?

lessons.aigp_text: Third-party components are part of the AI system an organization must govern, and NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function explicitly includes residual-risk monitoring and communication — which is what naming 'version-containment now, hash-verification as the explicit next step' performs: the treatment's boundary is on the record, not implied away. The NIST trustworthy-AI characteristic 'accountable and transparent' shows in the documentation choice — the rationale states what the control does not buy, so the next reader inherits the true posture.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: A version pin contains drift; only hash verification defeats substitution. Name the boundary every control actually buys.

lessons.title: Wiring a guard onto a hot path means asking what legitimate traffic it will see, not just what it is meant to catch.
lessons.failure: #634 asked for one line: register the exfil-screen — the filter that scans outbound data for secrets — onto the armed egress guard. Taken literally, it would have welded a noose. The guard screens every guarded send, and BlarAI's internal traffic (the loopback gateway hop, the Hyper-V vsock channel) legitimately carries the runtime's own capability tokens and the user's personal data — exactly what the screen flags. The first internal message would have tripped the kill-switch and cut all egress: a self-inflicted denial of service dressed as a security control. A second trap: the screen returns a Detection object, in Python always 'truthy' (it counts as true in a yes/no test), so the verdict normaliser would have read a clean Detection(blocked=False) as a positive hit and blocked the first clean external send too. Two independent ways the obvious wiring would have hung the system. Merged 651ef4a; gate green, 2372 passed.
evidence: 2026-06-09 — The screen that would have strangled the host it guards
commit 651ef4a
lessons.lesson: A content screen pointed at a channel that carries the system's own secrets will fire on them; a verdict contract read by truthiness will misread an always-truthy 'clean' result. Scope the guard to the traffic that actually crosses the boundary — here, only sockets that connect to an external-allowlisted endpoint are tagged for screening — and read verdicts by their explicit flag, never by truthiness.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: a detector is specified by what it should catch, but it is deployed onto a channel — and the channel's legitimate traffic decides its false-positive rate. A screen that flags credentials, pointed at the path that legitimately transports credentials, is an outage by design. Before arming any guard, enumerate two populations: what the boundary-crossing traffic looks like, and what flows on a normal day that must never trip it — then prove the allow side as rigorously as the deny side (this sharpens lessons 22 and 36). The second mechanism is contract reading: when one component interprets another's result, use the explicit named field, never an incidental property like truthiness — an object can be 'true' and mean 'clean.' Note that the existing seam tests had locked in the old broken behaviour; a passing test can guard a defect.
lessons.growth_question: Before a detector gets enforcement power over a live channel, which two populations of traffic must you enumerate — and which one decides whether the control survives its first day?

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF names 'safe' and 'secure and resilient' among its trustworthy-AI characteristics, and a control that denies the system's own legitimate operation fails the first while wearing the second. The MAP function asks for context: what the operating environment actually carries, not only what the threat model fears. Scoping the screen to boundary-crossing traffic — and proving it inert until an endpoint is allowlisted — exemplifies Domain III.A, governing the designing and building of the system so a protection cannot become the outage.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Scope guards to traffic that crosses the boundary and read verdicts by explicit flags; an unscoped screen is an outage wearing a security badge.

lessons.title: A merged capability is not an activated one - say which it is.
lessons.failure: The last two Lead-Architect-added gate items came back from their builders together: #637 rewrites how sensitive on-disk files get their permissions (Windows access-control lists); #639 wires a new branch into the authorization core — the ESCALATE consumer, the path that pauses a risky action for explicit operator approval. The merge-gate WAS the control: the review read the actual code and confirmed #639 fail-closed on every path (no verifier, an exception, a timeout, a malformed result all resolve to DENY) and dormant-safe (nothing registers at import). Then the honest framing for the gate record: the merge MERGED the consumer; it did not ACTIVATE it. An ESCALATE actually prompting the operator still requires registering a verifier at startup — a real behaviour change wanting a live end-to-end verify. Merged 2d82f69 and 494ebb8; the session's four merges took the standing gate from 2358 to 2444, each re-run on clean main.
evidence: 2026-06-09 — Merge-gating the last two Policy-Agent gate items
commit 2d82f69
commit 494ebb8
lessons.lesson: A control can be fully built, tested, and merged and still change nothing in production until a deliberate wiring step turns it on — the egress screen, the ESCALATE consumer. The honest gate record distinguishes 'the path exists and is proven' from 'the path is live,' because conflating the two is how a system gets credited for a protection it is not yet running.
lessons.growth: The conflation happens because the artifacts of 'done' look identical for both states: green tests, a merged branch, a closed ticket. A status vocabulary with only built and not-built loses the dimension that matters operationally — what runs. This sharpens lessons 23 and 46: a capability proven is not a control enforced, and tests prove a mechanism in isolation, never that the live system invokes it. The recovering question is cheap: for every merged control, ask what you would observe in production today that differs from yesterday; if the answer is nothing, write merged-dormant and schedule activation as its own deliberate, verifiable act. Notice also what made merging without a live-verify safe here: #637's permission helper grants the running account full access first, then locks others out — a control that can only tighten for others cannot brick the app. Find the invariant that bounds the blast radius.
lessons.growth_question: A security control merged today with all tests green: what single question tells you whether production is actually better protected tonight than it was last night?

fail-closed on every path"] B --> C["no verifier: DENY"] B --> D["exception or timeout: DENY"] B --> E["malformed result: DENY"] B --> F["explicit operator approval:

lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function covers implementing risk responses and communicating residual risk — and a gate record that writes merged-dormant instead of done is precisely that communication: the risk treatment exists but is not yet operating. The EU AI Act attaches record-keeping obligations to high-risk systems; the concept-level point is that records must reflect operational truth, not build status. Saying which exemplifies Domain III.C — governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Merged is a build status; active is an operational status. Gate records must say which, or protection gets credited before it runs.

lessons.title: An activated control is not an exercised one — verify it against the real producers, not assumed ones.
lessons.failure: #639 had a longer tail than its merge. The consumer went in dormant, and activating it was framed as a fresh decision to escalate — over-caution the Lead Architect punctured: 'why haven't you activated it?' Activation on the terminal surface turned out to be one line, because the orchestrator and the terminal app share a process. Then the live-verify 'didn't work,' and the failure was the real finding: none of BlarAI's four shipped tools (time, date, day-of-week, calculate) can produce an ESCALATE — the verdict that pauses a risky action for operator approval — at all. The seven ESCALATE rules key on file paths, large writes, and flags only a future file or network tool will carry. 'ESCALATE fires for seven rule classes today' was true of the rule logic, false of the current toolset. A deterministic demo (scripts/demo_escalation_modal.py) let the operator watch the real approval dialog approve and deny.
evidence: 2026-06-09 — The consumer that worked the moment it had something to consume
lessons.lesson: A consumer can be built, wired, activated, and even live-demonstrated and still never fire in production, because nothing yet produces its input. The honest record distinguishes 'the path works' from 'the path is exercised by real traffic,' and a live-verify must use the actual toolset — an assumed trigger, like a filesystem tool that does not exist, proves nothing.
lessons.growth: Controls form producer-consumer chains, and every link can be independently green while the chain has never carried a real event end-to-end. You verified the consumer with a synthetic trigger; production's job is to produce the trigger, and today it cannot. The recognition move: for any alarm, monitor, or approval path, ask what in the current system generates its input — and answer from the code, not the design document. Trace one real event from producer to consumer before claiming coverage; where no producer exists yet, record the control as live-but-dormant and name the event that will first exercise it. This sharpens lesson 46's 'built but wired into nothing' a full notch further: wired, activated, demonstrated — and still dormant in practice. The companion lesson is calibration: finishing a decision the operator already made is completion, not a new decision to hand back.
lessons.growth_question: The control is wired, activated, and just demonstrated live in front of the operator — what fact about today's toolset could still make 'it protects production' false?

lessons.aigp_text: This is the verification-versus-validation distinction inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function: the demo verified the mechanism was built to spec; validation asks whether it does the real job in the real context, and the real context contains no producer yet. Recording 'mechanism-verified, dormant until an escalating tool ships' exemplifies Domain III.C — governing release, monitoring and maintenance — where claims about a control's coverage are scoped to the traffic that actually exists.
