lessons.aigp_takeaway: Activated is not exercised: trace one real event from producer to consumer before claiming a control covers production.

lessons.title: A security control must not claim more than it delivers.
lessons.failure: A #611 live-memory feasibility study, grounding itself in the real cryptography code, found a control that lies about itself. The DEK break-glass recovery ceremony — the emergency path for recovering the data-encryption key (provision_dek_keystore.py) — 'zeroizes' recovered key material after use, except it doesn't. Every recovery_key = bytes(len(recovery_key)) rebinds the name to a fresh all-zero buffer; the original secret bytes object is immutable in Python and merely dereferenced, left in memory until the garbage collector reaps it. The comments ('overwrite', 'Zero out … in memory') asserted a residency control the code never delivered. The fix was deliberately the small one: correct the comments to state the real, weak guarantee — best-effort reference-drop, not in-RAM erasure — and point at #611 where the genuine improvement is tracked. The behaviour was deliberately NOT changed: making the code look like it zeroizes would re-assert the same false claim with more confidence.
evidence: 2026-06-09 — The zeroization that never cleared
lessons.lesson: When code documents a protection — zeroization, isolation, validation — the comment is a claim a future reader will trust. If the implementation cannot honour it (here, Python's immutability prevents true in-memory erasure), say so plainly and point at where the real mitigation is tracked. An overclaiming control is worse than an absent one, because it buys false confidence.
lessons.growth: Documentation and implementation drift independently, and security comments are the highest-stakes drift, because a reader's response to 'this is handled' is to stop checking — the overclaim converts directly into someone else's unexamined assumption. Note the second-layer trap this fix dodged: 'improving' the code cosmetically, with more elaborate-looking zeroization that still cannot erase immutable memory, would have re-asserted the false claim more convincingly. Sometimes the honest comment IS the fix, and the behaviour change belongs elsewhere, tracked. This sharpens lesson 47 (the comment that claimed a checksum the code never ran) and pairs with lesson 51 (make the property executable): where a property can be tested, encode it as a test that can fail; where the platform genuinely cannot deliver it, document the modest truth. Audit your comments as claims — for each 'this prevents, erases, or validates X,' ask what would make it false.
lessons.growth_question: A comment says the recovery key is 'zeroed out in memory' — what does the next maintainer now never check, and what happens if the claim is false?

lessons.aigp_text: Among NIST AI RMF's trustworthy-AI characteristics, 'accountable and transparent' covers the claims a system's builders make about their own controls — and an overclaiming comment is a transparency failure inside the codebase itself. The EU AI Act's high-risk obligations include technical documentation; the concept-level point is that documentation must match delivered behaviour. Correcting the claim to the modest truth, with the real mitigation tracked openly, exemplifies Domain III.A — governing how the system is built and described.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: An overclaiming control buys false confidence; document the guarantee actually delivered and track the real mitigation where reviewers can see it.

lessons.title: Strip authority with an allowlist, not a denylist — and know which lever you're pulling.
lessons.failure: BlarAI's launcher must run elevated because Hyper-V, Windows' virtual-machine layer, demands it — but "elevated" is not one bit. It is a bundle of ~twenty privileges stamped into the process token, Windows' record of what a running program may do: SeDebug, SeLoadDriver, SeTcb and more. The launcher is not just a launcher: it hosts the Policy Agent, the Assistant Orchestrator and the model as in-process threads, so every privilege it holds, the LLM (large language model) tool loop holds. The most security-sensitive component ran with all of admin by inheritance, chosen by no one. #652 strips the token down to a tiny keep-allowlist using SE_PRIVILEGE_REMOVED — deleted for the lifetime of the process, unrecoverable from inside — applied before any worker thread starts, so spawned children inherit the smaller token. The tempting design, a remove-list of known-dangerous privileges, was rejected: it fails open. On the elevated launcher the strip removes ~15 privileges.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — Taking away the keys the launcher never uses
lessons.lesson: Reduce a process's power by removing everything not explicitly needed, so an unanticipated capability is dropped by default instead of surviving a hard-coded remove-list: a denylist fails open, an allowlist fails safe, and "unknown means denied" is the only defensible default. And know which lever you are pulling: integrity-lowering (Windows' per-process trust ranking) cannot reach in-process threads; privilege-stripping shrinks what the High-integrity process may do without changing the integrity it runs at.
lessons.growth: Two mechanisms. A denylist encodes yesterday's threat inventory: it can only name the dangers its author knew, and the platform keeps minting capabilities — a new Windows build, a different elevation path — each surviving the list silently. An allowlist encodes need — small, stable, auditable; the unknown drops by default. Second, every control acts at a specific scope, and a control applied at the wrong scope does nothing while looking like hardening: lowering integrity is per-process, so it cannot touch workers living as threads inside a process that must stay High. When you shrink authority anywhere — permissions, API scopes, firewall rules — start from what is provably needed and drop the rest, then check what scope your lever actually acts at. Before each removal, ask which sibling control could fail silently without it: a fail-safe that skips looks exactly like one that ran, so keep on doubt and write the doubt down.
lessons.growth_question: When a capability you never anticipated shows up in the process, does your control drop it or keep it — and who chose that default?
lessons.aigp_text: Designing what authority an AI system inherits is part of governing how the system is built — AIGP Domain III.A. The stake is agentic: the AIGP Body of Knowledge v2.1 adds agentic architectures as deployment examples, and here the model's tool loop holds whatever the hosting process holds, so deny-by-default privilege design is model-and-system governance, not IT housekeeping. The allowlist posture exemplifies the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's "secure and resilient" trustworthy-AI characteristic and the OECD AI Principle of robustness, security and safety.

aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristic: secure and resilient
OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Authority an agentic system inherits is a governed design choice: allowlist what is needed, drop the unknown by default.

lessons.title: An audit log is the one at-rest store where signed-plaintext beats encryption.
lessons.failure: BlarAI's standing posture is encrypt-content-at-rest: the session and memory stores, sessions.db and substrate.db, are sealed under the DEK (data encryption key) per ADR-025, the at-rest encryption decision record (an ADR is an architecture decision record). The audit-retention work (#607) surfaced a question its builder was right to escalate rather than answer: the new sealed audit-log segments inherited the active log's on-disk shape — TPM-signed (Trusted Platform Module, a dedicated security chip), owner-only-DACL'd (a discretionary access control list, Windows file permissions), but plaintext. The reflex, and the project's standing lean, is encrypt-everything-at-rest; the builder had been overridden before for proposing a plaintext residual, so the honest move was to escalate with a recommendation rather than quietly ship either default. The operator ratified the recommendation as ADR-029: keep the audit log signed-and-locked, not encrypted — a genuine exception to a good default, not a lapse from it.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The one store I chose not to encrypt
lessons.lesson: An audit trail's value is independent verifiability: a reviewer — or a future operator — must verify it without holding the data-encryption key, and the trail must survive the loss or compromise of the very key whose use it records. Encrypting it couples the witness to the thing it witnesses. The exception holds because the records are metadata, not content — and it carries a named expiry: re-weigh when tools start writing sensitive paths or URLs into the log.
lessons.growth: Good defaults fail quietly when applied by category instead of by property. Encryption buys confidentiality; an audit log earns its keep through different properties — integrity, authorship, and the ability of someone without your keys to verify it after things go wrong — so the default optimizes the one property this store barely needs while taxing the ones it exists for. When you meet a store that resists your default, name the properties that store is for and check each control against them; a witness must never depend on the thing it witnesses. And handle the deviation as governance, not convenience: escalate it to the accountable owner with a recommendation, record the reasoning where the next reader will find it, and attach the condition that would reopen the question — an exception without an expiry quietly becomes policy.
lessons.growth_question: After the very key your system depends on is lost or compromised, which of your records could a reviewer still verify — and which died with it?
lessons.aigp_text: Record-keeping and logging are named high-risk obligation concepts in the EU AI Act because accountability rests on the trail — and a trail that dies with the key it monitors cannot support accountability when it matters most. The ratified deviation also exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function: a risk treatment chosen deliberately, its residual confidentiality exposure named rather than implied away, and a revisit trigger — sensitive identifiers entering the log — placed on the record as part of the decision.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — record-keeping/logging (high-risk obligation concept)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: An audit log must outlive the key it witnesses: sign and lock it, do not encrypt it — and record the exception's revisit trigger.

lessons.title: The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one.
lessons.failure: ADR-025 gave BlarAI at-rest encryption and honestly recorded the limit it could not cross: while the process runs, plaintext-derived secrets live in RAM. The #611 feasibility study walked that surface and named the worst offender — not the DEK (data encryption key) but the substrate's embedding cache: _load_embed_cache decrypts every document and turn vector (embeddings — numeric representations of text by meaning) at store construction and holds the lot for the store's lifetime. Embeddings are semantically invertible — vec2text-class attacks reconstruct text from them — making this the largest, longest-lived sensitive blob the running system held. It also exposed the existing key 'zeroization' as ineffective: rebinding a Python bytes name to zeros dereferences the secret but leaves its bytes in the heap until the garbage collector maybe reaps them. The fix (commit 295cb69) zeroes each cached numpy vector in place after an idle window, re-decrypting lazily on the next retrieval.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The secret you can actually erase
commit 295cb69
lessons.lesson: The only secret you can truly erase is a mutable one. Python bytes are immutable — rebinding the name to zeros dereferences the secret and leaves its bytes in the heap, so 'zeroization' of a bytes key is a comment, not a control. A numpy array is mutable, so an in-place overwrite genuinely scrubs the buffer. The honest test of any memory-residency claim: a captured reference to the buffer reads zero afterward — not merely that the container dropped it.
lessons.growth: Ineffective scrubbing survives because the code reads as if it worked: the variable now holds zeros, a test confirms the dictionary is empty, and nothing in the language's surface shows you the old bytes still sitting in process memory. Garbage-collected languages deliberately hide the storage layer, so 'I overwrote the secret' is a claim about objects, while attackers read memory. Before you claim erasure, ask two questions of the concrete type holding the secret: can its storage be overwritten in place at all, and what copies exist that you do not own — runtime internals, native libraries — that no in-place write reaches? Then write the test the property deserves: capture a reference to the buffer before the scrub and assert it reads zero after. And keep the claim honest about windows: erasure shrinks how long a secret is exposed; it cannot protect the moment it is in use.
lessons.growth_question: When your code 'zeroizes' a secret by assigning zeros to the variable, what happens to the bytes the variable used to point at?
lessons.aigp_text: The NIST AI RMF trustworthy-AI characteristic 'privacy-enhanced' has to hold in RAM as well as on disk: an invertible embedding cache is personal data in everything but format, and shrinking its resident window is privacy engineering at the memory layer. The captured-reference assertion is MEASURE-function TEVV (test, evaluation, verification, and validation) applied to the control itself — verifying the property actually claimed, erasure, rather than a proxy, emptiness — and the named irreducible in-use window models the honest residual-risk naming the framework expects.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Immutable secrets cannot be overwritten, only dereferenced; claim zeroization only where a captured reference provably reads zero afterward.

lessons.title: Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice
lessons.failure: Stage B of the #655 ingest program needed what BlarAI had avoided its entire life: a real HTML parser. The Lead Architect chose trafilatura — a library that extracts article text from web pages — at the cost of 9 hash-pinned packages (pinned to cryptographic checksums so only vetted bytes install); the package's own fetch functions were forbidden on arrival, enforced by a scan that fails the standing test gate on any reference. One ordering subtlety inside the pipeline earned its own test. The injection scan — the check for text crafted to smuggle instructions to the model — runs on normalized text BEFORE the delimiter strip. Strip first, and a forged <|GROUNDED_CONTEXT_BEGIN|> marker vanishes before the scanner sees it: the verdict gets laundered, and the operator reviews a 'clean' document that arrived armed. Normalization must precede both, because a zero-width space — an invisible character — inside 'ignore previous instructions' defeats a naive phrase scan.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The biggest dependency since OpenVINO, and the door we welded shut on arrival
commits 9121653, 7531abe
lessons.lesson: Sanitization order is a verdict-integrity property, not a style choice. Detect on what was actually submitted, then strip: strip-then-detect launders the quarantine verdict, because the evidence is destroyed before the detector runs. Normalize before detecting, because invisible characters are a scanner-evasion vector. Lock the ordering with tests that attack it — an evasion test proves the order does real work.
lessons.growth: A cleaning pipeline is a chain of transforms, and every transform destroys information; a detector placed downstream of a destructive transform can only judge what survived, so its verdict silently becomes a statement about the sanitized artifact, not the submitted one. The attacker controls the original; the reviewer's trust attaches to the verdict; ordering is the only thing connecting the two. You will find the same shape far beyond HTML: redaction before anomaly detection, markup stripping before moderation, decompression and resizing before forensic checks. The discipline: for every detector in a pipeline, name the artifact it must judge — usually as-submitted, after only the canonical normalization that removes disguises without removing evidence — then write an adversarial test that plants evidence exactly where a wrong order would destroy it, and keep that test as a permanent lock so a future refactor cannot quietly reorder the stages.
lessons.growth_question: If the delimiter strip runs before the injection scan, what artifact is the 'clean' verdict actually describing — and who downstream still believes it describes the document as submitted?
lessons.aigp_text: A quarantine verdict is an evaluation a human relies on to approve content into a decades-horizon store, and TEVV — test, evaluation, verification, and validation inside NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function — requires an evaluation to measure the artifact it claims to measure; strip-then-detect quietly substitutes a different artifact. Locking the order with adversarial tests, and keeping quarantined text visible so the operator's review stays meaningful, exemplifies Domain III.A — governing the designing and building of the system — and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Detectors judge only what survives upstream transforms: normalize, then detect, then strip — and lock the order with tests that attack it.

lessons.title: A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label
lessons.failure: An independent Lead-Architect-session verification of the knowledge-bank branch caught what its builder shipped without seeing. The knowledge_docs table stored the SHA-256 — a deterministic fingerprint: same input, same output, for anyone — of the full cleaned plaintext as a plaintext content_sha256 column, directly beside the AES-256-GCM ciphertext it fingerprints, and the signed-plaintext ingest audit log carried the same digest as car_hash. Every piece had a justification (the digest powers a staging integrity cross-check; the audit chain wants a content reference; 'it's only a hash'), and the composition was still wrong. Under the stolen-database threat model an attacker holding knowledge.db runs any public article through the deterministic in-repo cleaner, hashes the output, and tests membership — exactly the attack the keyed source_hash two columns away was designed to deny. The store denied 'did he ingest this URL' while answering 'did he ingest this article' for free.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The encrypted store that kept a plaintext fingerprint of its own contents
lessons.lesson: A deterministic hash of secret content is content metadata, not a label. Anywhere ciphertext is the design — at rest, or in a signed plaintext log — content-derived hashes must be keyed: an HMAC, a fingerprint that also requires a secret key, keeps equality checkable for the system and unguessable for a thief. 'It's only a hash' is the warning sign to check for an oracle; here the right pattern already sat two columns away, in the same CREATE TABLE.
lessons.growth: Why does this slip through? Each surface is judged against its own purpose — the integrity check, the audit reference — and each justification is locally true; the leak lives in the composition, plus an attacker capability the design never priced. Hashes feel safe because they are irreversible, but irreversibility only protects unguessable inputs: when the content is public or generatable, an attacker does not need to reverse your hash — only to recompute it and compare. Hunt for deterministic derivatives of protected data everywhere a system stores or logs them: digests, lengths, sort orders. For each, ask what an outsider who can generate candidate inputs could learn. The fixes are cheap when the read path never needs the raw value back: key the derivation — here the RAM-side integrity check was unchanged, cost zero — and add a regression lock that scans the raw stored bytes for the plaintext digest of a known item.
lessons.growth_question: The column is 'only a hash,' and hashes cannot be reversed — so what can an attacker who can fetch or guess candidate documents still learn from it, without reversing anything?
lessons.aigp_text: Membership inference — learning whether a specific item is inside a store — is a recognized privacy attack, and NIST AI RMF's privacy-enhanced trustworthy characteristic is judged against the composed system, not against each column's intent. The same verdict forced the data map honest: the claim of one deliberate plaintext metadata column was false, and the row now enumerates every plaintext column with a rationale. Pricing the attacker's recompute capability and documenting what is actually exposed exemplifies Domain III.A and the data-governance obligations the EU AI Act sketches for high-risk systems.

aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — data governance (concept level)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Deterministic derivatives of protected content answer membership questions; key them with a secret, and audit every 'only a hash' for an oracle.

lessons.title: When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature
lessons.failure: BlarAI was air-gapped by absence — no runtime module imported a network client — and the egress_guard, a socket-layer watchdog, welded that shut: any stray connect tripped a latched kill-switch. Then the Lead Architect decided to remove the air-gap, and the first feature wanting out was UC-003: read a URL, hand back cleaned text. The temptation was to let that feature open its socket; the build did the opposite — one door, guarded_fetch.fetch_external, four ordered fail-closed rules: an SSRF (server-side request forgery) check, the Policy Agent's per-URL verdict, a fetch inside a one-fetch, always-auto-revoked widening of the allowlist (the permitted-destination list), and an injection scan. Two choices carried the weight. The door ships with no adjudicator (verdict-giver) wired, so today every fetch DENIES. And consent is reserved for the ESCALATE tier — a fingerprint on every ALLOW trains rubber-stamping — recorded as an ADR-027 amendment, because it moves where the human gate sits.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The door we cut into the wall
commits b7471dd, 663c7fa
lessons.lesson: When you add the first instance of a capability a system was built to forbid, build the seam before the feature: one guarded door every future caller must pass through, never a socket per feature — and when the change moves where a human gate sits, encode it as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) amendment, not code alone. Ship the seam honest: with no real authority to consult, register nothing and fail closed — a documented TODO, never a fabricated verdict.
lessons.growth: A first instance sets the pattern: without a mandatory seam, every later feature opens its own socket, and the once-forbidden capability proliferates beyond any single point of policy, audit, or revocation. Build your choke point before the feature that needs it, so every future caller inherits the same ordered checks. Two corollaries travel with it. First, when no honest in-process authority exists to consult, wire nothing — a stub that answers ALLOW when nobody asked is a silent open door; the documented TODO is the fail-closed shape. Second, expect the fail-closed control to fight the legitimate use it must now permit: a real HTTP client connects to the numeric address a name resolved to, never the allowlisted name, which would have tripped the kill-switch on the first genuine fetch. Teach the control the legitimate path narrowly — pin the resolved address, at the allowlisted port, dropped on revoke — without loosening deny-by-default for anything else.
lessons.growth_question: The air-gap is coming down and the first network feature is ready to open its own socket — what must exist before that feature ships, and what does every later feature inherit from it?
lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function is the cross-cutting one — policies, accountability structures, risk culture — and a single sanctioned door is GOVERN made structural: one point where policy, audit, and revocation apply to every future network caller. Recording the consent posture as an ADR amendment, with the rejected blanket-consent alternative on the record, is the documentation the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept assumes: the record says where the human gate sits, and why. The work exemplifies Domain III.A — governing the designing and building of the AI system.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Introduce a forbidden capability through one governed door, fail closed where no authority exists, and record the human-gate placement as policy.

lessons.title: Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side
lessons.failure: The #655 ingest surface let the operator paste a web article into chat with /ingest, and the downstream pipeline did everything right: cleaned it, quarantined it, approval-gated it, encrypted it, and labeled it UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL. An independent Lead-Architect-session verification then found the channel built around every one of those defenses: the gateway (the component that parses chat commands) persisted the operator's full /ingest message — up to ~40 KB of raw, pre-cleaning web text — as a role='user' turn (a stored chat message attributed to the operator) in sessions.db, and the prompt-history filter forwards all user turns verbatim into later prompts. An identical uncleaned copy rode straight into the model's context as a trusted user turn on the next message — not a bypass of the defenses, a parallel path that never met them. The assistant-side preview was already correctly excluded: the reply was defended, the request left wide open.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The paste that walked in through the front door
lessons.lesson: Audit the request side of every interception seam, not just the reply side: a command surface that persists its own raw input as a user turn re-injects that input into future prompts with user-level trust, no matter how well the downstream pipeline guards the same content. The fix persists a labels-only stub — /ingest <article: {N} words, doc {uuid8}> — because absence cannot be forwarded; content-bearing markers rot and scope-creep.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: a new feature's defenses are built along its own forward pipeline, while the act of invoking the feature also rides a pre-existing channel — here the chat transcript — whose forwarding semantics were decided long before the feature existed. Every interception seam has two sides: the reply you return, and the residue the request itself leaves behind. When you add a command that carries content into a conversational system, trace where a copy of the raw input lands, what re-reads that store later, and with what trust level it re-enters. Then chase derived forms one level further: here even a fallback label leaked the content's full plaintext digest (its content fingerprint) into the transcript through the approval message. The durable lock is a dump-scan test — prove the content is absent from a full dump of the store, raw and cleaned forms both.
lessons.growth_question: The pipeline cleans, quarantines, encrypts, and labels the pasted article as untrusted — what happens to the chat message that delivered it, and what reads that message next?
lessons.aigp_text: NIST AI RMF's MAP function establishes context — the system, its data flows, and their risks — and this failure is a mapping gap: a copy of untrusted external content flowed on a path the risk treatment never covered. The trustworthy-AI characteristic at stake is privacy-enhanced; a trust label means little while an unlabeled copy travels in parallel. Auditing both sides of the seam and locking the absence with a dump-scan test exemplifies Domain III.A — governing the designing and building of the AI system across every path the data takes.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: Map every path data takes, not just the feature's pipeline; an ungoverned parallel copy defeats the governed one.

lessons.title: At a security boundary, prefer a wire format with exactly one legal encoding per payload
lessons.failure: BlarAI's design rule ADR-030 §3 promises that hostile fetched HTML is parsed inside the Hyper-V guest — an isolated virtual machine — never in the host process that holds the unsealed DEK (data-encryption key). The plumbing hit a hard number: one vsock frame (the host-to-VM socket transport) carries at most 65,536 bytes, while a fetched page is allowed 262,144 — four times the frame. Pages must cross as chunks, and the tempting scheme was the loose one: sender picks any chunk sizes, receiver accumulates until the declared total. Rejected — a loose receiver cannot distinguish a malformed sender from a hostile one. What shipped in shared/ipc/parse_channel.py is deterministic framing: every chunk except the last carries exactly 45,000 raw bytes (60,000 after base64, the bytes-as-text encoding), and the receiver validates each frame against its single legal size. Even 45,000 was earned: the 48,000 first budget overflowed on paper at the worst legal frame.
evidence: 2026-06-11 — The boundary earns its plumbing: a wire contract for hostile bytes
commits 16323cb, 23f667e
lessons.lesson: When the receiver can compute the single valid shape of every frame from the first frame's declaration, validation degenerates to equality checks, and every attack or corruption class becomes a first-frame or per-frame rejection. 'Tolerant reader' is a virtue in friendly protocols; at a security boundary it is a euphemism for unbounded trust. The 64 KB cap looked like an obstacle; it was the design pressure that made nothing about a message negotiable after its first frame.
lessons.growth: Why does looseness fail at a boundary? Every degree of freedom you grant a sender is state the receiver must tolerate without being able to judge — a loose accumulator cannot tell a malformed peer from a hostile one, so it extends trust it cannot verify, and accumulate-until-done is how unbounded-buffer bugs are born. When you design any interface that untrusted data crosses, remove degrees of freedom until exactly one legal encoding per payload remains: validation becomes equality, and rejection happens at the earliest possible frame — here, an oversize declaration dies on chunk 0, before any buffering. Two companion habits: do the worst-case arithmetic on paper, where the 48,000 budget failed before production ever could, then lock that arithmetic with a test asserting the worst legal payload fits the envelope. Constraints like a 64 KB frame cap are not obstacles; they are design pressure — use them.
lessons.growth_question: A receiver that tolerantly accepts whatever chunk sizes the sender chose — what can it no longer distinguish, and what does that cost at a hostile boundary?
lessons.aigp_text: Designing the channel that hostile content crosses is Domain III.A work — governing the designing and building of the AI system — and the deterministic wire contract exemplifies the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety: the boundary fails predictably, at the first bad frame, instead of tolerantly. The EU AI Act's high-risk obligations pair robustness with cybersecurity at concept level; a one-legal-encoding contract, documented so a later builder cannot mis-assume it, is that idea expressed as engineering.

lessons.aigp_takeaway: One legal encoding per payload turns boundary validation into equality checks; a tolerant reader at a hostile boundary is unbounded trust.
